title: On Being a Leader's Aide and Why That Is Not a Smaller Calling
excerpt: Support has been sold as second place. It is not. Some of the most powerful work done in the Kingdom has been done by the person standing behind the one at the front. And honestly, even that is not really the point.

body.text: Support has been sold as second place. It is not. Some of the most powerful work done in the Kingdom has been done by the person standing behind the one at the front. And honestly, even that is not really the point.

Because this is not ultimately about whether a calling is "big" or "small." That itself is already the wrong framework. The real question is this: Are you doing the will of the Father? That is the question. Not whether your position looks impressive. Not whether people applaud it. Not whether you are the visionary, the founder, the face, or the person whose name is remembered. The question is: Did you obey God?
The Burnings of My Heart Since Childhood

When I was a child, there were a few phrases I said often. One of them was: "Can I help?" Another was: "I just want to help people." And then there were the other two: "I want to be an entrepreneur." "I want to be an engineer."

Now at first glance those things may sound unrelated, but as I have gotten older, I have realized they actually had a common thread running through all of them. None of them were really about being "the main person." They were about building. Supporting. Helping something come forth. Almost like a midwife helping in a birth. Not the child itself. Not the mother herself. But still necessary to the process. That thread had always been there.

But there was another reality too. I struggled with diligence. I struggled deeply with consistency toward things I did not feel naturally connected to. If I could not immediately see how I would grow in something, or if the path looked impossible or unclear, I often lacked discipline toward it. I would do things merely to say I had done them. Not because I genuinely cared whether they were done well. And people noticed.

So the report surrounding my life became: "He is not hardworking. He is unfocused. He has no vision." People did not expect greatness from me. At best, they expected me to become some sort of general worker. And over time, even though part of me still held onto those burnings in my heart, another part of me slowly began believing their report. That is what repeated words can do if they are received deeply enough.
When Christ Began To Transform Me
Everything changed when I gave my life to Christ. As I sought Him sincerely, He began doing exactly what Scripture promises He does. He transformed me. Not instantly. Not artificially. But truly.
Do not be conformed to the pattern of this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind. Then you will be able to test and approve what God's will is, His good, pleasing and perfect will.

As I beheld Christ, I changed. Not because I was trying to imitate behavior externally, but because beholding Him began reshaping my inner nature. I saw holiness and began desiring holiness. I saw excellence and began moving toward excellence. The old people around me still held onto the old report, but new people entering my life could see something had shifted. They began saying things like: "You are growing in wisdom. You are ready for leadership. You are ready for ownership."

And because almost nobody had ever spoken good things over me before, I treasured those words deeply. But there was a problem. My mind had not yet been fully renewed. So when they said leadership, I imagined prominence. When they said ownership, I imagined position. And slowly, subtly, my expectation shifted.
Six Years. Twenty-Nine Tries.

For nearly six years, I chased leadership. Not necessarily out of rebellion at first, but because I genuinely believed that was what God had for me. My earliest jobs had "assistant" written directly into the role. And yet I still could not perceive what God was showing me. I was operating in my actual gifting. I was being trained in my actual calling. But because my expectation had shifted, I interpreted these roles as temporary waiting rooms instead of actual assignments.

So I kept trying to force myself into leadership. I started venture after venture. Twenty-nine different attempts over several years. Twenty-nine different efforts to create a leadership role for myself because I could not recognize that God had already positioned me elsewhere. And every time I forced it, there was resistance. Not harsh rejection. Just the Lord quietly saying: I am not in this.

And thank God for His mercy. Because if He had allowed me to succeed prematurely in my own ambition, I may have built an entire life outside His will while still using His name. That realization shook me deeply. You can do many things "for God" while not actually doing the will of God.
Mourning The Wrong Expectation

One of the hardest things I had to learn was this: Repentance is not always enough by itself. Sometimes you must also mourn. I had to mourn the version of my future that I had attached myself to. Because if you do not mourn old expectations properly, you will drag them into the next season. You will try to pour new wine into old wineskins. You will say you have surrendered while secretly still trying to preserve your original ambition.
To bestow on them a crown of beauty instead of ashes, the oil of joy instead of mourning, the garment of praise instead of a spirit of despair.

I had to grieve the image I had created of what significance was supposed to look like. Only then could I actually embrace the Lord's expectation. And when I finally did, something became shockingly clear. Every single door God had opened for me had been connected to support. Consulting. Teaching. Aiding. Helping establish others. That pattern had been there all along. I simply had not valued it. Because the world does not value it.
Joseph. Daniel. Mordecai. Esther.
Joseph was not Pharaoh. Daniel was not the king. Mordecai was not the monarch. And yet all of them fulfilled critical assignments in God's plan.
God sent me before you to preserve life.

Joseph preserved life during famine. Daniel revealed the wisdom and power of God before kings and empires. Mordecai protected and sought the welfare of God's people. Esther risked herself for deliverance at the appointed time. All of them occupied strategic positions. Not necessarily the highest positions. Strategic ones. And they were faithful there. That is the point.
And who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this?
The Chess Piece

Think about a chessboard. A pawn has little individual power compared to the other pieces. But in the hands of a skilled player, proper positioning changes everything. A well-positioned pawn can become critical to victory. The issue is not whether you are the biggest piece on the board. The issue is whether you are where the Master intended you to be. That is what obedience is. Positioning.

And many believers exhaust themselves trying to become queens when God assigned them elsewhere entirely. Not because He values them less. But because His wisdom sees the whole board while ours does not.
Doing Things In His Name vs Doing His Will
Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven.

That verse should terrify us in the healthiest way possible. Because it means you can build things in His name while still being outside His will. You can preach in His name. Lead in His name. Build ministries in His name. Start businesses in His name. And still miss the assignment entirely. That is why ambition must die. Not passion. Not diligence. Not excellence. Ambition. Vainglory. The desire to establish yourself apart from the exact will of God.
Little children, keep yourselves from idols, from anything and everything that would occupy the place in your heart due to God, from any sort of substitute for Him.
The Process of God

For those God foreknew He also predestined to be conformed to the image of His Son. And those He predestined, He also called; those He called, He also justified; those He justified, He also glorified.

Foreknown. Predestined. Called. Justified. Glorified. There is a process to God. There are things written concerning your life before you ever arrive on earth. The goal is not creating your own destiny. The goal is discovering and walking faithfully in the will already written by God. And that process requires renewal. It requires consecration. It requires allowing God to uproot false ambition. It requires patience. It requires trust. And most painfully sometimes, it requires letting go of what you wanted so that you can receive what He intended.
Behold, I have come. In the volume of the book it is written of me. To do Your will, O God.
What I Finally Understood

I finally understood that God was not withholding leadership from me. He was defining leadership differently than I had. Because true leadership in the Kingdom is not about status. It is about obedience. Sometimes leadership looks like pioneering publicly. Sometimes leadership looks like strengthening the hands of another. Sometimes it looks like consulting. Sometimes it looks like building systems quietly behind the scenes. Sometimes it looks like teaching and equipping others to execute what God gave them. And all of it matters if God ordained it.

The world glorifies visibility. But Heaven honors obedience. The Kingdom is not built by celebrities alone. It is built by faithful people. Faithful assistants. Faithful advisers. Faithful administrators. Faithful servants.
A Final Word

So if you are in a season where your role feels small, hidden, overlooked, or secondary, stop measuring your life through worldly rank. Ask instead: Lord, what is Your will for me? And once He reveals it, treasure it. Even if nobody else understands it. Even if it does not look impressive. Even if it does not satisfy human ambition.
I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith.

Paul could say that because he knew the assignment given to him by God, and he knew he had completed it. That is success. Not fame. Not status. Not visibility. Obedience. And honestly, that is enough.
